title: Run your first Selenium test
objective: Meet Selenium by running a real browser end to end: watch it open a page, find the heading, and read its text — then write the one line Selenium never writes for you, the check that the text is what you expected.
scenario: You've never used Selenium before. In front of you is a tiny signup page, a prepared TypeScript test that already opens a real browser and reads the page's heading, and a terminal to run it. Two of the three moves are done; your move is the last one — the check. There is no AI in this exercise: the one line is yours, written by hand, so you learn the shape of a Selenium test from the inside.

chat.goalPrompt: Hey! Sage here 🌿 — first time with Selenium, no worries. Before we open anything: in your own words, what do you think you're here to get done today?
welcome: This is a practice space — everything is local and disposable, and nothing you do here can break anything real.

Here's the shape of it: a Selenium test is three moves — OPEN a real browser, FIND an element and READ something off it, then CHECK that what you read is what you expected. The first two moves are already written for you.

Your move is the third: one line that checks the heading Selenium read matches the one you expected. The exact words are in README.md — no guessing spelling. Ask me anything along the way and I'll answer straight.

faq.answer: Move 2 already read the heading into a variable called headingText. Your line checks it against what you expected: assert.equal(headingText, "Community Garden Signup"). It reads out loud as "assert that headingText equals the heading I expected." That's the whole of move 3.

headingText is the variable move 2 filled in for you — it holds the words Selenium read off the page's heading (the <h1>). Your check compares THAT to the heading you expected to see. You don't re-find anything; you just check the value that's already there.

Your file is tests/first.test.ts — click it in Code Studio's file list on the left. Everything is prepared except the spot marked "MOVE 3", near the bottom of the try block. That empty spot is the only part that's yours.

Click once anywhere in the dark terminal area so it has the keyboard, type npm test, and press Enter. Selenium opens an invisible browser, runs your check, and prints PASS or FAIL. If the terminal fights you, "Check my work" also runs the test as part of verifying.

Selenium drives a real web browser from code — open a page, find things, click, read. Here it runs headless (invisible) so it's just fast. The one idea for today: Selenium reads the page for you, but it never decides whether what it read is correct — that check is always yours.
Yes — the Reset button is right next to "Check my work" at the bottom of this chat. It puts every file back exactly how it started (your edits are removed), and I'll stay right here.

tasks.title: Read the README
tasks.text: Open Code Studio (double-click its icon on the desktop), then click README.md in the file list on the left — it lays out the three moves of a Selenium test and the exact words you'll need for your one line.

tasks.title: See the visitor's page
tasks.text: Open Garden Site to look at the signup page the way a visitor does. Notice the heading at the top — that exact text, "Community Garden Signup", is what your check will confirm.

tasks.title: Read the two prepared moves
tasks.text: Open tests/first.test.ts and read moves 1 and 2 — the lines that open the browser (driver.get), find the heading (findElement), and read its text into headingText. Seeing what's already done is how you'll know exactly what your one line has to check.

tasks.title: Write move 3 — the check
tasks.text: In tests/first.test.ts, find the spot marked "MOVE 3" and TYPE one line: an assertion that headingText equals the heading you expected — "Community Garden Signup". Not sure of the words? README.md spells them out, or ask me. Save with Ctrl+S.

validate.criterion: At the spot marked 'MOVE 3' (after the getText/console.log lines, before the finally block), the learner has written an assertion that checks the variable headingText — the text read off the page — against the expected heading 'Community Garden Signup'. Accept equivalent spellings: assert.equal(headingText, 'Community Garden Signup'), assert.strictEqual(...), assert.ok(headingText.includes('Community Garden Signup')), or assert(headingText === 'Community Garden Signup'). NOT complete if: the MOVE 3 spot is still empty, the assertion does not reference headingText, it compares headingText to itself (e.g. assert.equal(headingText, headingText)), or it asserts something always-true. The prepared moves 1 and 2 (Builder/get/findElement/getText) do not count — only the learner's own check line does.

tasks.title: Run the test
tasks.text: Run `npm test` in the terminal and read what it prints — Selenium reports the heading it read, then PASS or FAIL. If the terminal fights you, "Check my work" runs your test too.

tasks.title: Check your work
tasks.text: When the test prints PASS and you can point at the line that does the checking, use "Check my work" — I'll verify your check is real (it catches empty and compare-to-itself checks, don't worry).

checkpoint.title: A real Selenium test, run end to end — with a check you wrote

requirements.label: The prepared moves (open, find, read) were left intact
Your check references the text read off the page (headingText)
It checks headingText against the expected heading (not itself)
Selenium runs the test and it passes
The page itself was left exactly as seeded
Workspace is in a valid state

instructorNotes: VOICE: informal, calm, brief, plain words — a colleague at the next desk. Audience: an absolute beginner with NO Selenium and possibly no automated-testing experience at all; this is an INTRO lesson, the gentlest rung. THE ONE LESSON: a Selenium test is three moves — OPEN (driver.get), FIND & READ (findElement → getText into headingText), then CHECK (an assertion the learner writes). The distinctive Selenium idea vs. tools they may meet later: Selenium reads the page for you but never decides correctness — the check is always the human's job. SCOPE: keep it to moves 1-3 on THIS page. Do NOT introduce CSS-selector theory, XPath, explicit/implicit waits, page objects, clicking/forms, or Playwright comparisons unless the learner asks — none belong in this intro. NEVER write or paste the assertion for them; the single line is theirs to author (instructor-takeover is a scenario critical failure). Coach with questions first: 'move 2 already read the heading into headingText — what do you want to compare it against?'. Common beginner slips (expected, not failures): (a) writing assert.equal(headingText, headingText) — ask what that actually proves if the page were wrong; (b) believing PASS alone means done before they've written a real comparison — the verifier catches empty/self-referential checks with teaching details, so trust it and never claim completion yourself. Vocabulary to introduce gently only after the plain version: assertion ('the line that says what should be true'), headless ('the browser runs invisibly'). There is deliberately no AI surface here; if asked, say this one is hand-written on purpose so the three-move shape sticks.
